text: Over the last few days, Google rolled out some serious updates. Liria 3 is now generating full music with vocals inside Gemini. Pomelli is adding AI powered product photo shoots for businesses. And Stitch just introduced new agents that design apps, generate app store assets, and plug straight into code editors. A lot is moving at once, so let's break it down. All right, so Google has officially launched Lyria 3, its newest music generation model, and this one is a real step forward compared to what they showed last year. Liria 3 is rolling out inside the Gemini app and it is also powering Dream Track, which is part of YouTube's creator toolkit. This is not some researchonly demo anymore. This is Google pushing music generation directly into consumerf facing products that millions of people already use. At a basic level, Lyria 3 lets you generate 30-second music tracks using natural language prompts. You can describe a genre, a mood, the tempo, and even the language of the lyrics. And importantly, you no longer need to bring your own lyrics. Lia 3 generates lyrics automatically along with vocals and instrumentation. floor. That's a big usability jump over LIA 2, which launched last May and still required more manual input. What makes this more interesting is that text is not the only input. You can upload an image or a video, and Lyria 3 will generate a track that matches the visual content. That tells you immediately that Google is treating music as a firstass modality alongside text and vision. Audio is no longer an afterthought in the Gemini ecosystem. On the technical side, LIA 3 generates audio at a 48 kHz sample rate using 16bit PCM stereo output. That's real production quality audio, not some compressed demo format. The output length is currently capped at 30 seconds in the Gemini app, but the quality and complexity of the music are noticeably higher than before. These are full arrangements with multiple instruments and vocals, not stitched loops. Under the hood, Google is solving a hard problem here. Music is continuous and multi-layered. You're dealing with melody, harmony, rhythm, tambber, and long range coherence all at the same time. A song has to sound like the same song from the first second to the 30th second. LIA 3 is designed specifically to handle that. It generates music from scratch rather than assembling pre-made components. Google has not fully disclosed the architecture, but they do explain that different music models take different approaches. Some generate spectrograms first, which are visual representations of sound. Others, like Google's open- source music ML, work with compressed audio tokens. LIA 3 sits in this newer generation of models that can handle highfidelity audio directly while maintaining structure over time. Now, there's another layer here that matters a lot, especially for creators and platforms. Every track generated by LIA 3 contains an imperceptible watermark created by Google's synth ID technology. This watermark is embedded

this newer generation of models that can handle highfidelity audio directly while maintaining structure over time. Now, there's another layer here that matters a lot, especially for creators and platforms. Every track generated by LIA 3 contains an imperceptible watermark created by Google's synth ID technology. This watermark is embedded cannot hear it, but software can detect it. Even if the audio is compressed into MP3, slowed down, or recorded through a microphone, the watermark remains. Users can check whether a track contains synth ID by uploading it back into the Gemini app. This is Google taking attribution and safety very seriously. Music generation raises obvious copyright concerns and synth ID is their technical answer to that problem. Instead of relying on metadata that can be stripped out, they're embedding a digital signature into the sound itself. And because LIIA 3 can generate music directly from images and video, audio and visuals are starting to collapse into a single creative workflow. Now, Higsfield is sponsoring today's video, and they've recently rolled out Cinema Studio 2, which is built specifically for that kind of multimodal production. Instead of treating AI video as a simple prompt and weight tool, Higsfield is structured like a real studio pipeline. You plan shots, control the camera, and generate scenes with intention. With Cinema Studio 2, you start by locking in a hero frame, then define camera movement, lens behavior, focal length, and timing before generation begins. That setup keeps lighting, composition, and subjects consistent once motion starts, which is still one of the biggest challenges in AI video. You can also sequence multiple shots in one project, so outputs feel like a directed scene rather than random clips. All of this sits inside a single platform that already hosts many of the newest and strongest AI video models in one place. Instead of forcing you to jump between tools, Higsfield has also confirmed that Cedance 2 is coming soon, adding another high-end generation engine focused on long- form, cohesive video. And right now, they're running an exclusive Cling 3 campaign, giving everyone 50% off access to Cling 3, which is one of the most impressive video models available today. So, as music, visuals, and direction merge into one workflow, Higsfield is building the layer that actually makes it usable. Take a look and try it. Links in the description. LIIA 3 is currently available to adult users on the Gemini mobile app. Google has already said that the desktop version is coming in the next few days. Usage caps depend on your subscription tier with higher limits for Google AI Plus Pro and Ultra subscribers. And while there is no public API yet, Google has openly hinted that LIIA 3 could eventually become accessible through an application programming interface just like other Gemini powered models. If that happens, this becomes much bigger than a consumer toy. It turns into infrastructure. There is also a real-time side to LIIA 3 that deserves attention. Google DeepMind has

openly hinted that LIIA 3 could eventually become accessible through an application programming interface just like other Gemini powered models. If that happens, this becomes much bigger than a consumer toy. It turns into infrastructure. There is also a real-time side to LIIA 3 that deserves attention. Google DeepMind has time. Instead of the classic prompt and weight workflow, this system operates on a chunk-based auto reggressive stream. Audio is generated in two-second chunks over a birectional websocket connection. The model looks backward at previous context to maintain the groove and forward at user controls to adjust style and direction. This allows for live steering using weighted prompts. You can change the mood or instrumentation while the music is playing and the model adapts in real time. Latency for control changes is under 2 seconds. That's a very different creative experience compared to static generation. On top of that, Google has built what they call the music AI sandbox. This is aimed at musicians and creators who want more hands-on control. You can take a simple hum or a basic piano line and turn it into a full orchestral arrangement. You can use MIDI chords to generate vocal choirs. You can change instruments with text prompts while keeping the same melody. This is human in the loop AI where the model becomes something you jam with rather than something you just query. Now, naturally, this puts Google directly into competition with companies like Sunno and UIO. Sunno raised $250 million last November and focuses on catchy, viral friendly music with features like multi- stem track splitting. Udio leans towards studiograde fidelity and advanced editing including inpainting and long- form tracks up to 15 minutes with extensions. Google's current limitation is the 30-second cap, but their strength is speed, multimodal input, and tight integration with Gemini. And that's the key pattern here, integration. While LIIA 3 handles music, Google is also expanding visual generation and design tools in parallel. This brings us to Pomelli. Pomelli is Google's AI marketing experiment from Google Labs. Built with help from DeepMind, it launched in October 2025 as a public beta focused on small and medium-siz businesses. It's currently free and available across four English-speaking markets. Since launch, Google has been quietly adding features. And now, Pamelli is preparing to roll out something called Photoshoot. Photoshoot is designed to solve a very practical problem. Professional product photography is expensive. studios, lighting, photographers, post-processing, stock licensing. All of that adds up quickly. Photoshoot lets businesses upload an image of a physical product, choose from a set of visual themes, select templates that match those themes, and then generate polished marketing images as if the product went through a professional photo session. These images slot directly into Pomelli's existing campaign flow. Pomelli already uses something called a business DNA profile which captures a brand's identity, tone, and visual style. Photoshoot builds on top of that foundation and produces ready to use assets for social media, advertising,

went through a professional photo session. These images slot directly into Pomelli's existing campaign flow. Pomelli already uses something called a business DNA profile which captures a brand's identity, tone, and visual style. Photoshoot builds on top of that foundation and produces ready to use assets for social media, advertising, platform. This is clearly aimed at e-commerce sellers, product brands, and small retailers who don't have access to professional photography at scale. And it fits into a broader pattern. In January 2026, Pomellle added animate through VO3.1 integration, allowing animated assets. Photoshoot follows the same layering approach, adding another generative capability on top of brand identity and campaign workflows. Pomelli is still officially in public beta, but given how fast Google has been iterating, a near-term release of photo shoot seems likely. Google is positioning Pomelli as a serious contender in SMB marketing automation, not just an experiment. Now let's shift to design and agents. Stitch is Google's AI design tool. It launched at Google IO 2025 as a rebranded version of Galileo AI. Since then, Google has been steadily expanding its capabilities. Stitch already supports Figma export across all agents and earlier this month, Google rolled out the IDed agent for early stage exploration. Now in the latest development builds, a new agent called Hatter has appeared. Hatter is described as an agent that can create highquality designs. Right now, when triggered, it behaves similarly to the standard design flow, but the labeling matters. Google is calling it an agent, not a model. That suggests it's meant to handle more complex multi-step design tasks over time rather than just generating a single output. This likely ties into something called deep design, which has been spotted before. Think of it as a design focused counterpart to deep think. Applying deeper reasoning to UI and layout generation. Instead of just producing screens, an agent like Hatter could reason through flows, constraints, and design systems stepby step. Alongside Hatter, two other features have surfaced in Stitch Builds. The first is app store asset generation. This allows users designing mobile apps to automatically generate store ready screenshots with descriptions and an app icon. For indie developers and small teams, this is a huge timesaver. You can go from prototype to app store visuals without switching tools or hiring a designer. The second is native MCP integration. MCP stands for model context protocol and in this case, Google is building it directly into Stitch's export menu. Previously, developers relied on thirdparty connectors like Lovable. With native MCP, Stitch can generate an API key and connect directly to tools like Cursor, Claude, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI. Communitybuilt MCP bridges already exist on GitHub, but a firstparty implementation lowers the barrier significantly. Designers and developers can pull Stitch design straight into their coding environments with minimal friction. And one more detail that often gets overlooked, Google is doing all of this while keeping latency low. Liria Realtime operates with under two seconds

on GitHub, but a firstparty implementation lowers the barrier significantly. Designers and developers can pull Stitch design straight into their coding environments with minimal friction. And one more detail that often gets overlooked, Google is doing all of this while keeping latency low. Liria Realtime operates with under two seconds directly into coding tools. Pomelli generates assets directly inside campaign workflows. So what we're seeing here is Google moving from model announcements to system building. Music, images, design, agents, and deployment are starting to blur into one continuous creative stack. Not everything is fully exposed yet. APIs are still limited. Track length is still capped. Some agents are clearly early. But the direction is very deliberate. That's where things stand right now. If you want more breakdowns like this, hit subscribe and drop a like. Thanks for watching and I'll catch you in the next one.

title: Google Just Dropped LYRIA 3: New AI Feature No One Expected
